title: Strings
summary: Create, index, compare, and combine text.
refresher: `std::string` stores text, `.size()` reports its character count, and indexing starts at zero. Strings support comparison and concatenation with compatible operands.

blocks.body: `std::string` stores text, `.size()` reports its character count, and indexing starts at zero. Strings support comparison and concatenation with compatible operands. Create, index, compare, and combine text.

`std::string` manages a sequence of characters and its storage for you. A `char` holds one character, while a string can grow and provides methods such as `.size()`, `.substr()`, and `.find()`. Indexing is unchecked with brackets, so stay inside the valid range.

blocks.caption: Substring takes a starting index and a character count, unlike APIs that use an ending index.
Check that text exists before reading its final character.
blocks.wrong: char last = text[text.size() - 1]; // unsafe when empty

blocks.body: For a nonempty word, `back()` reads the final character. For an empty string, there is no such character, so the condition skips access. The size is a count, not an index: a four-character string has valid positions zero through three.

blocks.title: C++ detail to check
blocks.body: For a nonempty string, the final character is at `text.size() - 1`; using `text[text.size()]` steps past the valid range.

blocks.why: The string contains three characters, so its size is three.
The call starts at index one and takes two characters, producing od.

blocks.title: Strings checklist
items: Check emptiness before using the last character.
